questionText: Disprove the statement: ``The sum of any two consecutive prime numbers is even.''

steps.description: Read the claim as a universal statement.
steps.workingLatex: \forall\ \text{consecutive primes } p, q:\ p + q \text{ is even}
steps.explanation: The claim asserts the sum is *always* even; one consecutive pair with an odd sum disproves it.
We need a single pair of consecutive primes whose sum is odd.

steps.description: See why it usually looks true.
steps.explanation: Most primes are odd, and two odds sum to an even number, which makes the claim seem right — but $2$ is an even prime.
The very first consecutive prime pair is $2$ and $3$, and $2$ is the exceptional even prime.
There is no prime strictly between $2$ and $3$, so they are genuinely consecutive primes.
Their sum is $5$, which we now test for parity to challenge the claim.
Since $5$ is odd, this pair of consecutive primes breaks the claim.
Because one of the primes is the even prime $2$, the sum is even $+$ odd $=$ odd, unlike the odd+odd case.
The consecutive primes $2$ and $3$ sum to $5$, which is odd, so the claim is disproved.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: Take the consecutive primes $2$ and $3$: their sum is $5$, which is odd. Hence the statement is false. $\blacksquare$
